metadata.title: Maximum Points You Can Obtain from Cards

learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Sliding Window, Prefix Sum, Loops, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine choosing souvenirs from a shelf where you can only take items from the left or right end. You want to maximize the total value after picking exactly k items.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine choosing souvenirs from a shelf where you can only take items from the left or right end. You want to maximize the total value after picking exactly k items.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Complement Sliding Window pattern whenever you need to select elements from both ends while leaving one contiguous middle segment.
learning.signals: Whenever elements can only be chosen from both ends and exactly k selections are required, think about leaving one contiguous middle segment and solve it using Sliding Window.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: There are several cards arranged in a row, and each card has an associated number of points. You can pick exactly k cards. In each step, you can take one card from either the beginning or the end of the row. Return the maximum score you can obtain.

examples.explanation: Choose cards with values 6, 5, and 1 for a total of 12.

edgeCases: Maximum score comes entirely from one side

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Recursively try every possible combination of taking cards from the left and right.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Instead of choosing k cards from the ends, think of leaving behind n-k consecutive cards in the middle. Minimize the sum of those remaining cards, then subtract it from the total sum.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Selecting k cards from both ends is equivalent to removing one contiguous subarray of length n-k.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever elements can only be chosen from both ends and exactly k selections are required, think about leaving one contiguous middle segment and solve it using Sliding Window.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Selecting k cards from both ends is equivalent to leaving one contiguous middle subarray of size n-k. Finding the minimum such subarray using Sliding Window gives the maximum obtainable score in linear time.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever elements can only be chosen from both ends and exactly k selections are required, think about leaving one contiguous middle segment and solve it using Sliding Window.
commonWrongPaths: Forgetting that exactly k cards must be chosen.
Not handling k equal to array length.
Using a maximum instead of a minimum window.

hints: Instead of selecting k cards, remove n-k cards.
Use a sliding window to find the minimum middle sum.
commonMisconceptions: Forgetting that exactly k cards must be chosen.
Not handling k equal to array length.
Using a maximum instead of a minimum window.

pattern.name: Complement Sliding Window
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever elements can only be chosen from both ends and exactly k selections are required, think about leaving one contiguous middle segment and solve it using Sliding Window.

approaches.title: Try Every Combination
approaches.idea: Recursively try every possible combination of taking cards from the left and right.
algorithm: Repeat until k cards are chosen.

code.java: Recursive solution omitted due to exponential complexity.

approaches.title: Prefix and Suffix Sums
approaches.idea: Precompute prefix and suffix sums, then try every possible split between left and right picks.
approaches.dryRun: Combine i cards from the left and k-i cards from the right.

approaches.title: Complement Sliding Window
approaches.idea: Compute the total sum of all cards. Find the minimum sum subarray of length n-k using a sliding window. The answer equals totalSum minus this minimum window sum.
approaches.intuition: Selecting k cards from both ends is equivalent to leaving one contiguous middle subarray of size n-k. Finding the minimum such subarray using Sliding Window gives the maximum obtainable score in linear time.
algorithm: If k equals array length, return total sum.
Slide the window while maintaining the minimum window sum.
approaches.dryRun:
cardPoints=[1,2,3,4,5,6,1], k=3
Total=22
Window Size=4
Window Sums:10→14→18→16
Minimum=10
Answer=22-10=12

visualization.description: Leave the smallest middle window and keep everything else.
steps: Create a window of size n-k.

interviewFollowUps: Why is finding the minimum middle window equivalent?
What if cards can only be picked from one side?
Can the picked indices be returned?
How would the solution change for variable k?
interviewTips: Convert the problem into finding the minimum window.
Explain why window size is n-k.
State O(n) time and O(1) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Selecting k cards from both ends is equivalent to removing one contiguous subarray of length n-k.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Total Sum → Minimum Window (n-k) → Total - Minimum.

importantPoints: Minimum middle window gives maximum score.
commonMistakes: Forgetting that exactly k cards must be chosen.
Not handling k equal to array length.
Using a maximum instead of a minimum window.

youtube: NeetCode - Maximum Points You Can Obtain from Cards
Nick White - Maximum Points from Cards
